questions.question: Define e-waste and list any four categories of e-waste according to WEEE Directive.
keyPoints: Definition: Discarded, surplus, obsolete electrical/electronic devices
Temperature exchange equipment (refrigerators, ACs)
Screens and monitors
Lamps (fluorescent, LED)
Large appliances (washing machines)
Small equipment (vacuum cleaners, toasters)

questions.question: Explain the environmental and health impacts of informal e-waste recycling.
keyPoints: Exposure to toxic substances (lead, mercury, cadmium)
Respiratory problems from burning plastics
Neurological damage and kidney failure
Water and soil contamination
Children and pregnant women most vulnerable

questions.question: What is Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) and how does it help in e-waste management?
keyPoints: Policy approach making producers responsible for end-of-life products
Financial and operational responsibility for collection/recycling
Encourages design for recycling
Creates funding mechanism for proper disposal
Reduces burden on municipalities and consumers

questions.question: Discuss the Basel Convention and its relevance to e-waste management.
keyPoints: International treaty adopted in 1989
Controls transboundary movements of hazardous wastes
Aims to prevent illegal e-waste export from developed to developing countries
Requires prior informed consent before waste shipment
Promotes environmentally sound disposal

questions.question: What is urban mining? Explain its significance in sustainable resource management.
keyPoints: Process of recovering valuable materials from e-waste
Extracts gold, silver, copper, rare earth elements
Reduces need for primary mining (environmentally damaging)
Conserves natural resources
Creates green jobs in recycling sector

questions.question: Explain the global e-waste statistics and trends as per the Global E-waste Monitor 2024.
keyPoints: 62 million tonnes generated in 2022
Only 17% formally recycled
China leads in e-waste generation (10.1 million tonnes)
E-waste growing faster than formal recycling capacity
Projected to reach 82 million tonnes by 2030

questions.question: Differentiate between formal and informal e-waste recycling methods.
keyPoints: Formal: regulated, safe, proper infrastructure
Informal: unregulated, unsafe, primitive techniques
Formal recovers fewer materials but safely
Informal recovers more economically but with health/environmental damage
Most developing countries rely on informal sector

questions.question: Describe the global scenario of e-waste generation, trade, and management. Discuss the challenges faced by developing countries in managing e-waste.
keyPoints: Global e-waste generation: 62 million tonnes (2022)
Major generating countries: China, USA, India, Japan
Only 17% formally recycled globally
E-waste trade from developed to developing nations
Illegal exports disguised as donations
Basel Convention limitations
Developing countries lack infrastructure
Informal recycling causes health/environmental issues
Lack of regulations and enforcement
Need for international cooperation and technology transfer

questions.question: Discuss the environmental and health hazards associated with e-waste.Suggest suitable measures for safe e-waste management.
keyPoints: Hazardous substances: lead, mercury, cadmium, chromium, brominated flame retardants
Health impacts: neurological damage, respiratory problems, kidney failure, cancers
Environmental impacts: soil/water contamination, air pollution
Measures: Proper collection mechanisms, formal recycling facilities
EPR implementation, public awareness, stricter regulations
Technology transfer to developing nations, international cooperation
Design for environment, green computing initiatives

questions.question: Explain the concept of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) and discuss e-waste management regulations in India.
keyPoints: EPR definition and principles
Producer responsibility for collection/recycling
Financial mechanisms for e-waste management
Indian E-Waste Management Rules 2016 (amended 2018)
Collection targets and producer obligations
Role of dismantlers and recyclers
Authorization and compliance requirements
Challenges in implementation
Comparison with EU WEEE Directive

questions.question: What is the need for sustainable e-waste management? Discuss the principles of 3R (Reduce, Reuse, Recycle) in e-waste management.
keyPoints: Need: Rapid e-waste growth, environmental hazards, resource conservation
Reduce: Design for longevity, eco-labeling, responsible consumption
Reuse: Refurbishing, resale, donation programs
Recycling: Material recovery, urban mining, closed-loop systems
E-waste hierarchy preference: prevention > reuse > recycling > disposal
Benefits: Resource conservation, reduced pollution, green jobs
Challenges: Collection rates, informal sector, consumer awareness
